perturbation.original_text: 5.1 ebix shall pay to About, [**] in consideration for the Advertising Impressions as set forth in Section 4.1 [ADVERTISING IMPRESSIONS; INTEGRATION IMP...], herein, no later than seven (7) business days from the Effective Date , which shall be paid to About via wire transfer, pursuant to the following instructions:[**]
perturbation.changed_text: 5.1 ebix shall pay to About, [**] in consideration for the Advertising Impressions as set forth in Section 4.1 [ADVERTISING IMPRESSIONS; INTEGRATION IMP...], herein, no later than ninety (90) business days from the Effective Date , which shall be paid to About via wire transfer, pursuant to the following instructions:[**]
perturbation.explanation: Extended the payment deadline to 90 days, which could conflict with prompt payment laws in some states or industries if such a long delay is deemed unreasonable. This delay may violate standard business practice and related regulations.
perturbation.law_explanation: NY GBL § 511 requires that payments be made in accordance with the terms of the agreement or within a reasonable time if no terms are specified. Delaying payment to 90 days after the effective date for advertising impressions may be deemed unreasonable, especially considering standard business practices for advertising services, thus contradicting the spirit and potential enforcement of this law.
perturbation.justification: The modified text extends the payment deadline from seven to ninety days, which creates a legal contradiction. The Law Explanation states that NY GBL § 511 requires payments to be made within a reasonable time if no terms are specified, and that delaying payment to 90 days after the effective date may be deemed unreasonable. The Perturbation Explanation also points out that a 90-day delay may conflict with prompt payment laws or violate standard business practice. Scraped Law Snippet 1, although incomplete, mentions NYSenate.gov and links to legislation, suggesting the existence of laws governing business practices in New York. Because the change introduces a potentially unreasonable delay, and potentially conflicts with prompt payment laws, a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: 13.2 Either party may terminate this Agreement if the other (i) fails to make any payment required to be made by it hereunder for more than twenty (20) business days after such party shall have received notice from the other party of the failure of payment thereof, or (ii) commits a material breach of this Agreement that is not cured within thirty (30) calendar days after receipt of written notice of the breach.
perturbation.changed_text: 13.2 Either party may terminate this Agreement if the other (i) fails to make any payment required to be made by it hereunder for more than five (5) business days after such party shall have received notice from the other party of the failure of payment thereof, or (ii) commits a material breach of this Agreement that is not cured within sixty (60) calendar days after receipt of written notice of the breach.
perturbation.explanation: Reduced the cure period for payment failure to 5 business days, while extending the cure period for other material breaches to 60 days.  The shortened cure period for payment defaults might violate certain state laws regarding reasonable time to cure breaches of contract, particularly if the amount due is in dispute.
perturbation.law_explanation: CCP § 1161 outlines the notice requirements for unlawful detainer actions in California, often related to lease agreements.  While this agreement isn't a lease, the principle of 'reasonable notice' to cure a breach, especially for payment, is generally applied. A 5-day cure period for non-payment might be considered unreasonably short in some circumstances, especially compared to the extended 60-day cure period for other breaches, potentially conflicting with the implicit requirement of reasonableness under similar legal principles.

(Amended (as added by Stats. 2020, Ch. 37, Sec. 16) by Stats. 2024, Ch. 287, Sec. 6.)
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces an inconsistency regarding time-sensitive obligations. The original contract stipulated a 20-day cure period for payment failures, which was reduced to 5 days in the changed text. While the provided law snippet (CCP § 1161) specifically addresses unlawful detainer actions, the Law Explanation highlights the general legal principle of 'reasonable notice' to cure a breach, especially for payment. The LLM explanation suggests that the reduced 5-day cure period might be considered unreasonably short, potentially conflicting with this implicit requirement of reasonableness. By significantly shortening the cure period for payment defaults, the changed text creates a condition that might be deemed legally insufficient. This aligns with the definition of 'Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction,' where a deadline violates state law.

perturbation.original_text: 5.4 About shall have the right to examine, or to have examined by a representative of About, ebix's books and records to verify the accuracy of payments made to About for a maximum period of last 6 months preceding a written notice of About, pursuant to this Agreement. About shall provide ebix with at least thirty (30) days' prior notice of an audit and such audit shall be conducted at ebix's offices for a maximum period of two business days, during regular business hours, subject to ebix's cooperation.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.4 About shall have the right to examine, or to have examined by a representative of About, ebix's books and records to verify the accuracy of payments made to About without any limit to the period preceding a written notice of About, pursuant to this Agreement. About shall provide ebix with at least five (5) days' prior notice of an audit and such audit shall be conducted at About's offices for an unlimited period of time, during regular business hours, without ebix's cooperation.
perturbation.explanation: Removed the 6-month limitation on the audit period, reduced the audit notice to 5 days, moved the audit location to About's offices, extended the audit period to unlimited and removed requirement for ebix's cooperation. Removing restrictions on the audit scope and location could violate state laws regarding reasonable business practices or breach of contract, especially if the contract is governed by a state like Illinois or New York that emphasizes good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.law_explanation: While this is a service agreement, not a sale of goods, UCC § 2-302, which addresses unconscionable clauses, provides a relevant analogy. The modified audit clause, with its unlimited scope, short notice, change of location, long duration and lack of requirement for cooperation, could be deemed unconscionable or violate the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, especially if enforced aggressively. The changes significantly disadvantage ebix and grant About an unreasonable level of control and access.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-302. Unconscionable contract or Clause. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-302. Unconscionable contract or Clause. (1) If the court as a matter of law finds the contract or any clause of the contract to have been unconscionable at the time it was made the court may refuse to enforce the contract, or it may enforce the remainder of the contract without the unconscionable clause, or it may so limit the application of any unconscionable clause as to avoid any unconscionable result. (2) When it is claimed or appears to the court that the contract or any clause thereof may be unconscionable the parties shall be afforded a reasonable opportunity to present evidence as to its commercial setting, purpose and effect to aid the court in making the determination. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified contract significantly alters the original agreement regarding audit rights, potentially violating the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, as explained in the Law Explanation. The original contract limited the audit period to 6 months, required 30 days' notice, specified the audit location as Ebix's offices, limited the audit duration to two business days, and required Ebix's cooperation. The changed text eliminates the audit period limitation, reduces the notice to 5 days, moves the audit to About's offices, allows for an unlimited audit period, and removes the requirement for Ebix's cooperation. The UCC § 2-302, addresses unconscionable clauses and while it is not directly applicable, the LLM uses it as an analogy to demonstrate that the modifications could be deemed unconscionable because they grant About an unreasonable level of control and access, thus creating a significant imbalance and disadvantaging Ebix.
